prompt.content: Using network_outages.csv and customer_complaints.csv (you should use Python just to copy these files into the new Excel sheet), create an Excel workbook named outage_complaint_tracker.xlsx for the operations team. Keep one raw data tab for each file, then build an Outage_Tracker sheet that includes all network_outages rows and adds new Excel columns: outage_day (date derived from start_time), complaints_48h (count of complaints whose complaint_date falls on outage_day or the next day), and high_complaint_flag (1 if complaints_48h is at least 3, else 0). In a small Summary section on the same sheet, report the total number of outages in 2024, the percent of 2024 outages that are flagged high_complaint_flag, and the outage_id with the highest complaints_48h. Add a column chart that shows the count of 2024 outages by complaints_48h bucket, using buckets 0, 1-2, 3-5, and 6-9 (x axis = bucket label, y axis = outage count). Format the percent to two decimals and give the completed Excel file.

rubrics.name: Reports the total outages for 2024 to be exactly 124. (ver_d83d3186b62d403b8f056d19c60978f7)

messages.content: Reports the total outages for 2024 to be exactly 124.

rubrics.name: Reports the percentage of outages that have high complaints in 2024 as 23.39%... (ver_6aa769913a3b4bf5b6b4505339679fd6)

messages.content: Reports the percentage of outages that have high complaints in 2024 as 23.39% (+/- 0.03).

rubrics.name: Reports the outage_id with the most complaints in 2024 as 313. (ver_380e7798efe3472fb4af85d04a32f4d4)

messages.content: Reports the outage_id with the most complaints in 2024 as 313.

messages.content: The x axis represents the bucket label.

messages.content: The y axis represents the outage count.

messages.content: The column chart with the bar representing 0 complaints_48h has an outage count of exactly 20 (acceptable range 19.8 to 20.2).
